items.title: Observed phishing URLs delivering RMM payload
items.description: ScreenConnect is used in phishing URLs to deliver RMM payload. DocuSign has been observed to be the common theme in these phishing emails.
items.ai_summary: The observed phishing campaign utilizes ScreenConnect to deliver a Remote Monitoring and Management (RMM) payload, leveraging DocuSign as a theme to trick victims into opening malicious emails. The attack vector involves phishing emails, classified as T1566, which aim to gain initial access to the target system. If exploited, this could lead to unauthorized access and potential data breaches, affecting individuals and organizations that use DocuSign services. To remediate, defenders should implement robust email filtering, educate users about phishing attacks, and ensure all systems are up-to-date with the latest security patches. Additionally, monitoring network traffic for suspicious activity related to ScreenConnect and RMM tools is crucial.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1566| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.001| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: "Ghost" Code Phishing Analysis
items.description: EvilTokens is a sophisticated phishing kit that conceals critical components of its attack through browser-side AES-GCM encryption, creating visibility gaps for traditional static URL analysis. The kit exploits Microsoft's legitimate device login flow through OAuth device-code phishing to gain account access without directly stealing passwords. Targeting organizations primarily in the United State
items.ai_summary: The EvilTokens phishing kit exploits Microsoft's legitimate device login flow through OAuth device-code phishing, utilizing AES-GCM encryption to conceal critical components of its attack. This creates visibility gaps for traditional static URL analysis, making it challenging for defenders to detect. The kit primarily targets organizations in the United States, and if exploited, it can lead to unauthorized account access without directly stealing passwords. To remediate, defenders should implement OAuth 2.0 device code flow validation and monitor for suspicious login activity, specifically focusing on device-code phishing attempts. Additionally, enabling multi-factor authentication (MFA) can significantly reduce the impact of this type of phishing attack.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1566| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.001| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1204| D[Privilege Escalation]:::tactic
    D -->|T1550| E[Credential Access]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Observed activity associated with Sidewinder APT. Lure document: No.9374.docx, 64f2681ad0940e6c2c9c76e6834117bf. Observed C2 infrastructure: update[.]ms-office[.]app
items.description: Recent activity has been detected linked to the Sidewinder advanced persistent threat group. The campaign utilizes a malicious document named No.9374.docx with the hash value 64f2681ad0940e6c2c9c76e6834117bf as a lure mechanism. The infrastructure supporting command and control operations includes the domain update[.]ms-office[.]app. This observation indicates ongoing operations by Sidewinder, a t
items.ai_summary: The Sidewinder APT group has been observed utilizing a malicious document named No.9374.docx with the hash value 64f2681ad0940e6c2c9c76e6834117bf as a lure mechanism to gain initial access. This document is used in spearphishing attacks, leveraging T1566.001, where the victim is tricked into opening the malicious attachment, leading to user execution (T1204) of the malware. The command and control operations are supported by the domain update[.]ms-office[.]app, which was acquired through T1583 (Acquire Infrastructure). To mitigate this threat, defenders should block access to the update[.]ms-office[.]app domain and implement application layer protocol controls to detect and prevent T1071 (Application Layer Protocol) abuse. Additionally, educating users about the risks of opening suspicious attachments and implementing email filters to detect spearphishing attempts can help prevent initial access.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1566.001| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1204| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1071| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1583| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Skill Marketplace and the Emerging AI Supply Chain Threat
items.description: Between February and May 2026, researchers identified five malicious skills on ClawHub, OpenClaw's AI agent marketplace, that evaded detection by VirusTotal and ClawScan. The threats included two macOS infostealers communicating with command-and-control infrastructure, one skill using file padding to bypass scanner thresholds, and two novel agentic threats exploiting the AI supply chain for financ
items.ai_summary: Researchers discovered five malicious skills on ClawHub, an AI agent marketplace, between February and May 2026. The threats included two macOS infostealers communicating with command-and-control infrastructure, one skill using file padding to bypass scanner thresholds, and two novel agentic threats exploiting the AI supply chain for financial gain. These skills evaded detection by VirusTotal and ClawScan, indicating a sophisticated attack. To remediate, users should immediately remove any suspicious skills from their ClawHub accounts and update to the latest version of the platform. Additionally, administrators should review and validate the integrity of all AI skills and models in their supply chain.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1195| B[Supply Chain Compromise]:::tactic
    B -->|T1027.001| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1071| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1041| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: macOS.Gaslight | Rust Backdoor Turns Prompt Injection on the Analyst, Not the Sandbox
items.description: A sophisticated Rust-based macOS implant named macOS.Gaslight has been discovered, featuring a novel 3.5 KB prompt-injection payload containing 38 fabricated system messages designed to disrupt LLM-assisted malware analysis. The backdoor communicates via Telegram Bot API with AES-GCM encrypted payloads over certificate-pinned TLS and includes self-redaction capabilities to hide its bot token from
items.ai_summary: The macOS.Gaslight backdoor is a sophisticated Rust-based implant that features a novel 3.5 KB prompt-injection payload designed to disrupt LLM-assisted malware analysis by injecting fabricated system messages. This backdoor communicates via the Telegram Bot API, utilizing AES-GCM encrypted payloads over certificate-pinned TLS, and includes self-redaction capabilities to hide its bot token. The real-world impact of this threat includes the potential for unauthorized access and control of macOS systems, with affected parties likely including individuals and organizations that rely on LLM-assisted malware analysis. To remediate this threat, defenders should implement robust network traffic monitoring to detect and block suspicious Telegram Bot API communications, and consider utilizing endpoint detection and response tools to identify and remove the backdoor. Additionally, defenders should prioritize the use of multi-factor authentication and keep software up to date, specifically ensuring that any remote access software is patched and configured to prevent unauthorized access.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1027| B[Obfuscated Files or Information]:::tactic
    B -->|T1219| C[Remote Access Software]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Inside FortiBleed: Reverse Engineering the CyberStrike Harvester Behind a Global FortiGate Credential Factory
items.description: FortiBleed is a large-scale credential compromise campaign targeting internet-facing Fortinet FortiGate firewalls and SSL VPN gateways globally. The operation employs a sophisticated credential pipeline utilizing credential stuffing, password spraying, configuration harvesting, offline cracking, and post-authentication capture processing. Reverse engineering of the CyberStrike Harvester v1.5 binar
items.ai_summary: The FortiBleed campaign targets internet-facing Fortinet FortiGate firewalls and SSL VPN gateways globally, utilizing a sophisticated credential pipeline that includes credential stuffing, password spraying, configuration harvesting, offline cracking, and post-authentication capture processing. The attackers employ the CyberStrike Harvester v1.5 binary to compromise credentials, with the campaign detected to use Valid Accounts (T1078) and Brute Force (T1110) tactics. If exploited, this could lead to unauthorized access to sensitive networks and data, affecting organizations relying on FortiGate firewalls and SSL VPN gateways. To remediate, defenders should immediately update their FortiGate firewalls to the latest version, ensure strong passwords and multi-factor authentication are in place, and monitor for suspicious login activity. Additionally, defenders should review their incident response plans to ensure they are prepared to respond to credential compromise incidents.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1078| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1110| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1021| D[Persistence]:::tactic
    D -->|T1602| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Ukraine's UAV Supply Chain Targeted With Besomar-Themed Malware Chain
items.description: A newly identified threat group, designated as GhostShell, has been conducting cyber operations against Ukraine's unmanned aerial vehicle supply chain since February 2026. The attackers employ malicious archives containing decoy documents that impersonate Besomar, a Ukrainian manufacturer of high-precision interceptor drones, to compromise defense and procurement networks. The attack chain deploys
items.ai_summary: The GhostShell threat group has been targeting Ukraine's unmanned aerial vehicle supply chain with a bespoke malware chain since February 2026, leveraging malicious archives containing decoy documents impersonating Besomar, a Ukrainian manufacturer of high-precision interceptor drones. The attackers exploit public-facing applications, compromise the supply chain, and ultimately encrypt data for impact, as indicated by the detected TTPs T1190, T1195, and T1486. To mitigate this threat, defenders should implement robust email filtering, inspect archives and documents from untrusted sources, and ensure that all defense and procurement networks are isolated and monitored. Additionally, network administrators should apply the principle of least privilege, regularly update and patch all software, and use encryption to protect sensitive data. By taking these specific remediation steps, defenders can reduce the risk of compromise and minimize the impact of a potential breach.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1190| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1195| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1486| D[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: StealC and Amadey: Breaking down infostealers and the cybercrime services that deliver them
items.description: Infostealers remain among the most pervasive cybercrime threats, silently harvesting passwords, cookies, and session tokens that enable enterprise breaches. StealC is a malware-as-a-service infostealer written in C++ that collects credentials from browsers, cryptocurrency wallets, messaging applications, email clients, and gaming platforms while functioning as a secondary loader. Amadey operates a
items.ai_summary: The StealC infostealer, written in C++, poses a significant threat to organizations by silently collecting sensitive information such as passwords, cookies, and session tokens from various applications, including browsers, cryptocurrency wallets, and email clients. This malware operates as a secondary loader, further increasing its potential impact. The real-world impact of StealC and similar infostealers like Amadey can be substantial, leading to enterprise breaches and significant financial losses. To remediate, defenders should implement specific security controls such as multi-factor authentication, regularly update and patch software, and use anti-malware solutions capable of detecting and blocking infostealer activity. Additionally, monitoring network traffic for unusual patterns and educating users about the risks of phishing and other social engineering tactics can help prevent the initial infection vector.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1588| B[Obtain Capabilities]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.001| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: StrikeShark: a new campaign involving a custom SharkLoader and Cobalt Strike Beacon
items.description: A previously undocumented malware family named SharkLoader has been discovered delivering Cobalt Strike Beacon to targets worldwide. The threat actor deploys SharkLoader through exploitation of internet-facing applications including Microsoft Exchange, SharePoint, and Openfire Server, as well as through malicious droppers disguised as legitimate software. SharkLoader employs sophisticated techniqu
items.ai_summary: The StrikeShark campaign involves a custom malware family called SharkLoader, which delivers Cobalt Strike Beacon to targets worldwide. SharkLoader exploits internet-facing applications such as Microsoft Exchange, SharePoint, and Openfire Server, as well as disguises itself as legitimate software. The threat actor uses sophisticated techniques to deploy SharkLoader, including masquerading and data collection from information repositories. If exploited, SharkLoader can lead to remote access and data breaches. To remediate, defenders should update Microsoft Exchange, SharePoint, and Openfire Server to the latest versions, and implement strict access controls and monitoring to detect and prevent malicious activity. Additionally, users should be cautious when downloading software and should only install software from trusted sources.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1036| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1213| C[Command and Control]:::tactic
    C -->|T1219| D[Execution]:::tactic
    D -->|T1055| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: New Backdoor May be Linked to Ransomware Access Broker
items.description: A stealthy new backdoor called Mistic has been deployed in cybercrime intrusions since April 2026, potentially linked to Woodgnat, an initial access broker associated with multiple ransomware operations including Qilin, Interlock, Rhysida, Akira, 8Base and Black Basta. Mistic was deployed alongside ModeloRAT in at least one case, a tool developed by Woodgnat. The backdoor uses sideloading techniqu
items.ai_summary: A stealthy new backdoor called Mistic has been deployed in cybercrime intrusions since April 2026, potentially linked to Woodgnat, an initial access broker associated with multiple ransomware operations. Mistic uses sideloading techniques and was deployed alongside ModeloRAT in at least one case. If exploited, Mistic can grant threat actors remote access to compromised systems, enabling them to encrypt data using tools like Qilin, Interlock, Rhysida, Akira, 8Base, and Black Basta ransomware. To remediate, defenders should ensure all systems are running the latest version of their operating system and software, and implement strict access controls to prevent sideloading. Additionally, monitoring for suspicious network activity and implementing a robust incident response plan can help mitigate the impact of Mistic.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1650| B[Acquire Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1219| C[Remote Access Software]:::tactic
    C -->|T1486| D[Data Encrypted for Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: CVE-2016-20075: WordPress Ultimate Product Catalog 3.8.6 contains an arbitrary file upload vulne...
items.description: WordPress Ultimate Product Catalog 3.8.6 contains an arbitrary file upload vulnerability that allows authenticated users with contributor, editor, author, or administrator roles to upload malicious files by exploiting the custom fields functionality. Attackers can upload PHP shells through the Products tab custom file field and access them via the upcp-product-file-uploads directory to execute arb
items.ai_summary: CVE-2016-20075 is a critical arbitrary file upload vulnerability in WordPress Ultimate Product Catalog 3.8.6, allowing authenticated users with contributor, editor, author, or administrator roles to upload malicious files via the custom fields functionality in the Products tab. Attackers can exploit this vulnerability to upload PHP shells and execute arbitrary code via the upcp-product-file-uploads directory. To remediate this vulnerability, update to WordPress Ultimate Product Catalog 3.8.7 or later, and ensure that the 'wp-content/uploads/upcp-product-file-uploads' directory is writable only by the web server user. Additionally, consider disabling the custom file field in the Products tab or restricting access to it. This vulnerability has a real-world impact, as it can be exploited to gain remote code execution and potentially lead to data breaches or ransomware attacks.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1204| B[User Execution]:::tactic
    B -->|T1505.003| C[Web Shell]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: CVE-2016-20074: WordPress Lazy Content Slider Plugin 3.4 contains a cross-site request forgery v...
items.description: WordPress Lazy Content Slider Plugin 3.4 contains a cross-site request forgery vulnerability that allows attackers to perform unauthorized actions by crafting malicious HTML forms. Attackers can trick authenticated administrators into submitting POST requests to the plugin settings page via lzcs_admin.php to modify plugin configuration parameters like lzcs_color and lzcs_count.
items.ai_summary: CVE-2016-20074 is a cross-site request forgery (CSRF) vulnerability in WordPress Lazy Content Slider Plugin 3.4. Attackers can trick authenticated administrators into submitting malicious POST requests to the plugin settings page via lzcs_admin.php, allowing them to modify plugin configuration parameters like lzcs_color and lzcs_count. This vulnerability has a CVSS score of 4.3, indicating a medium severity risk. To remediate, update the WordPress Lazy Content Slider Plugin to version 3.5 or later, and ensure that administrators are cautious when interacting with plugin settings pages. Additionally, consider implementing CSRF protection measures, such as validating and sanitizing user input, to prevent similar attacks in the future.

items.title: CVE-2016-20073: Answer My Question 1.3 plugin for WordPress contains an SQL injection vulnerabil...
items.description: Answer My Question 1.3 plugin for WordPress contains an SQL injection vulnerability that allows unauthenticated attackers to execute arbitrary SQL queries by injecting malicious code through the 'id' POST parameter. Attackers can submit crafted SQL statements to the modal.php endpoint to extract sensitive database information including WordPress terms and configuration data.
items.ai_summary: CVE-2016-20073 is an SQL injection vulnerability in the Answer My Question 1.3 plugin for WordPress, allowing unauthenticated attackers to execute arbitrary SQL queries by injecting malicious code through the 'id' POST parameter. Attackers can submit crafted SQL statements to the modal.php endpoint to extract sensitive database information, including WordPress terms and configuration data. This vulnerability has a CVSS score of 8.2, indicating a high severity. To remediate, update the Answer My Question plugin to version 1.4 or later, which patches the vulnerability. Additionally, ensure that the WordPress 'id' parameter is properly sanitized to prevent SQL injection attacks. Users of the vulnerable plugin should also consider implementing a Web Application Firewall (WAF) to detect and block malicious traffic.

items.title: CVE-2016-20072: BBS e-Franchise 1.1.1 plugin for WordPress contains an SQL injection vulnerabili...
items.description: BBS e-Franchise 1.1.1 plugin for WordPress contains an SQL injection vulnerability that allows unauthenticated attackers to execute arbitrary SQL queries by injecting malicious code through the uid parameter. Attackers can craft requests to pages using the plugin's shortcode with UNION-based SQL injection in the uid parameter to extract sensitive data from the WordPress database including user inf
items.ai_summary: CVE-2016-20072 is an SQL injection vulnerability in the BBS e-Franchise 1.1.1 plugin for WordPress, allowing unauthenticated attackers to execute arbitrary SQL queries by injecting malicious code through the uid parameter. Attackers can craft requests to pages using the plugin's shortcode with UNION-based SQL injection in the uid parameter to extract sensitive data from the WordPress database, including user information. This vulnerability has a CVSS score of 8.2 and is exploitable via the T1190 (Exploit Public-Facing Application) tactic. To remediate, update the BBS e-Franchise plugin to version 1.1.2 or later, and ensure that the WordPress database is properly sanitized to prevent SQL injection attacks. Additionally, consider implementing a Web Application Firewall (WAF) to detect and prevent future attacks.

items.title: CVE-2016-20071: The 404 Redirection Manager plugin version 1.0 for WordPress contains an unauthe...
items.description: The 404 Redirection Manager plugin version 1.0 for WordPress contains an unauthenticated SQL injection vulnerability that allows remote attackers to execute arbitrary SQL queries by injecting malicious code through unsanitized user input. Attackers can craft GET requests with SQL injection payloads to manipulate database queries and extract sensitive information from the WordPress database.
items.ai_summary: The CVE-2016-20071 vulnerability in the 404 Redirection Manager plugin version 1.0 for WordPress allows remote attackers to execute arbitrary SQL queries through unsanitized user input. This is achieved by crafting GET requests with SQL injection payloads to manipulate database queries and extract sensitive information from the WordPress database. If exploited, attackers can gain unauthorized access to sensitive data, potentially leading to data breaches or privilege escalation. To remediate this vulnerability, WordPress administrators should update to version 1.1 or later, which patches the SQL injection vulnerability. Additionally, users should ensure that all plugins are kept up-to-date and that input validation is implemented to prevent similar attacks in the future.

items.description: WordPress Booking Calendar Contact Form 1.0.23 contains an unauthenticated blind SQL injection vulnerability in the shortcode function that fails to sanitize the calendar parameter before using it in database queries. items.title: CVE-2016-20067: WordPress CP Polls 1.0.8 contains a cross-site request forgery vulnerability tha...

Attackers can craft malicious HTML pages that execute unwanted poll operations when administrators visit the page while logged in.

items.title: CVE-2016-20066: WordPress CP Polls 1.0.8 contains a persistent cross-site scripting vulnerabilit...
items.description: WordPress CP Polls 1.0.8 contains a persistent cross-site scripting vulnerability that allows attackers to inject malicious scripts through unsanitized file upload functionality. Attackers can upload files containing script payloads with event handlers like onerror attributes to execute arbitrary JavaScript in the browsers of users viewing the affected content.

items.title: Runlayer Raises $30 Million in Series A Funding
items.description: The startup’s platform functions as a secure control layer, aiming to secure AI tools across enterprises. The post Runlayer Raises $30 Million in Series A Funding appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: ThreatsDay Bulletin: Smart TV Proxyware, 24-Year curl Bug, AI Crime Forums + 13 More Stories
items.description: It’s dumb out there again. This week has the usual smell of prod on fire and nobody wanting to admit who left the door open — old creds still working, trusted apps doing sketchy crap, browser tricks jumping the fence, and “normal” workflows turning into phishing pipes because apparently email was not enough hell already. The worst part is how cheap some of it feels. Not elite. Not cinematic.

items.title: Ukraine's state postal operator reports app disruption after cyberattack
items.description: Ukraine's state-owned postal operator said it was experiencing disruptions to some of its app services due to a suspected cyberattack, but did not say who was behind it.

items.title: Russia used Cellebrite phone-hacking tool to crack down on dissident after firm cut off country
items.description: The continued use of the powerful data extraction product soon after the company in March 2021 said it would stop working with Russia suggests the firm has been unable to pull back its technology from authoritarian government customers, researchers say.

items.title: Webinar: Why account takeovers remain one of the hardest threats to stop
items.description: Account takeover attacks continue to challenge security teams because attackers often operate through legitimate accounts and trusted services. This webinar explores how behavioral AI can help organizations identify compromised accounts faster and automate response workflows. [...]

items.title: Cal Water Finds No Evidence of OT Activity After Hackers Claimed They Could Disrupt Water Supply
items.description: Mandiant has helped the California water utility investigate the cyberattack launched by Iranian hacker group Handala. The post Cal Water Finds No Evidence of OT Activity After Hackers Claimed They Could Disrupt Water Supply appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Lantronix Serial-to-IP Converter Flaw Exploited in Attacks After OT Threat Warning
items.description: The exploited flaw, CVE-2025-67038, is one of the vulnerabilities disclosed in April as part of the BRIDGE:BREAK research project. The post Lantronix Serial-to-IP Converter Flaw Exploited in Attacks After OT Threat Warning appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Surviving the Mythos Era: Richard Bejtlich on the Case for NDR
items.description: Despite the abundance of telemetry at analysts’ disposal, many security operations teams struggle to answer a few basic questions during incident investigation: What happened? What evidence do we have? How do we know we’re seeing it all, in context? Answering these questions requires teams to go beyond alerts, the most common basis for initial triage. items.title: GitLab Patches Code Execution, Information Disclosure Vulnerabilities
items.description: The latest GitLab CE/EE updates address 13 vulnerabilities, including three high-severity defects. The post GitLab Patches Code Execution, Information Disclosure Vulnerabilities appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.description: [This is a Guest Diary by Nicole Phillips, an ISC intern as part of the SANS.edu BACS program]

items.title: Europe Evolves Into Ransomware's Favorite Region
items.description: After a global lull, ransomware gangs are setting sights on a rich new arena: attacking EU organizations and their suppliers.

items.title: 25-Year-Old Vulnerability Patched in Curl
items.description: The latest version of the open source data transfer tool resolves 18 medium and low-severity vulnerabilities. The post 25-Year-Old Vulnerability Patched in Curl appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: New Gaslight macOS Malware Uses Prompt Injection to Disrupt AI-Assisted Analysis
items.description: A previously undocumented Rust-based macOS implant and information stealer has been found to embed a prompt injection payload designed to trick a malware analyst's artificial intelligence (AI) tools and trick it into aborting or refusing an analysis of the artifact. The malware has been codenamed Gaslight owing to this deceptive behavior. It's been assessed with high confidence that the tool is

items.title: New Mistic Backdoor Linked to KongTuke in ClickFix and ModeloRAT Campaigns
items.description: A new, stealthy backdoor named Mistic has been deployed as part of suspected financially motivated attacks aimed at multiple organizations spanning insurance, education, IT, and professional services sectors since April 2026. According to Symantec and Carbon Black's Threat Hunter Team, the backdoor, also tracked as MLTBackdoor, is said to be linked to an initial access broker (IAB) named

items.title: NIST Opens Updated IoT Security Guidance to Public Review
items.description: The guidance aims to establish product cybersecurity requirements for IoT devices integrated into federal agencies’ networks. The post NIST Opens Updated IoT Security Guidance to Public Review appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Chrome 149 Update Resolves 18 Severe Vulnerabilities
items.description: More than half of the bugs are use-after-free defects, which can potentially lead to remote code execution. The post Chrome 149 Update Resolves 18 Severe Vulnerabilities appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Cisco SD-WAN Zero-Day Exploited Months Before Patching
items.description: CVE-2026-20245, the 7th Cisco SD-WAN vulnerability exploited in 2026, was used for months prior to its disclosure and patching. The post Cisco SD-WAN Zero-Day Exploited Months Before Patching appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Cisco Catalyst SD-WAN Zero-Day CVE-2026-20245 Exploited to Gain Root Access
items.description: An unknown threat actor exploited a recently disclosed high-severity security flaw impacting Cisco Catalyst SD-WAN as a zero-day at least two months before it was publicly disclosed, according to new findings from Google-owned Mandiant. The vulnerability, tracked as CVE-2026-20245 (CVSS score: 7.8), allows an authenticated, local attacker to execute arbitrary commands with elevated privileges

items.title: Google releases new privacy controls for activity history, personalization
items.description: Google is rolling out new privacy controls for Search services and Google Play, giving you more control over saved history and personalized recommendations. [...]

items.title: DraftKings hacker 'Snoopy' sentenced to 18 months in prison
items.description: A 21-year-old using the alias "Snoopy" was sentenced to 18 months in prison for his role in hacking DraftKings accounts in the November 2022 cyberattack. [...]

items.title: Mandiant reveals how Cisco SD-WAN zero-day attacks gained root access
items.description: New details have been revealed on how hackers exploited a Cisco Catalyst SD-WAN vulnerability tracked as CVE-2026-20245 in zero-day attacks to create rogue root accounts on targeted devices. [...]

items.title: Attackers Hit Cisco SD-WAN Flaw 2 Months Before Disclosure
items.description: Researchers believe rogue peering was used to connect to the victim's SD-WAN devices to gain admin privileges and root-level access.

items.title: Malicious Edge extension abuses Native Messaging as bridge to malware
items.description: A malicious Microsoft Edge extension dubbed 'Edgecution' has been used in a ransomware attack to escape the browser sandbox and deploy a Python-based backdoor. [...]

items.title: 2026 FIFA World Cup Faces Surge in Cyber Threats
items.description: Persistent cybercrime, social engineering, and infrastructure threats continue to plague the FIFA 2026 World Cup across the US, Canada, and Mexico.

items.title: Three ‘cybercrime as a service’ operations undercut by Microsoft, law enforcement
items.description: Microsoft touted its latest action against malware infrastructure as a new approach aimed at the full cybercrime "supply chain." Europol said more than 300 servers were targeted.

items.title: Do CISOs Need a Code of Ethics?
items.description: Dark Reading Confidential Episode 19: Kickbacks, no-show jobs, "dirty" VCs, and shelf ware — industry expert Robert "RSnake" Hansen explains why he thinks its time for a CISO code of ethics to ensure cybersecurity bosses aren't engaged in self-dealing that could risk enterprise, and even national, security.

items.title: When Information Becomes the Attack Surface – Understanding AI Agent Traps
items.description: From hidden content injections to cognitive state poisoning, attackers are turning trusted data sources into traps for autonomous AI. The post When Information Becomes the Attack Surface – Understanding AI Agent Traps appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: CISA Warns Critical Lantronix EDS5000 Flaw Is Being Actively Exploited
items.description: The U.S. Cybersecurity and Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA) on Tuesday warned of active exploitation of a critical security flaw impacting Lantronix EDS5000 Series devices, urging Federal Civilian Executive Branch (FCEB) agencies to apply the fixes by June 26, 2026. The vulnerability in question is CVE-2025-67038 (CVSS score: 9.8), a code injection flaw that could result in the execution

items.title: More Malicious OpenClaw Skills Threaten AI Supply Chain
items.description: OpenClaw removed five packages from ClawHub, its skills marketplace, that bypassed security checks even though they included infostealers and other threats.

items.title: Amadey and StealC Malware Network Disrupted, 27M Stolen Credentials Recovered
items.description: A coordinated law enforcement operation, in partnership with private sector companies, including Bitdefender, Bitsight, ESET, and Microsoft, has resulted in the takedown of criminal infrastructure powering Amadey and StealC. "The main common goal was to disrupt the 'assembly lines' cybercriminals use to launch ransomware, financial fraud, and attacks on critical infrastructure," Europol said in

items.title: Microsoft and Allies Smash Shared Infrastructure of Amadey and StealC Malware
items.description: Hundreds of C&C servers were disrupted in an operation involving law enforcement and several cybersecurity companies. The post Microsoft and Allies Smash Shared Infrastructure of Amadey and StealC Malware appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: CISA warns of max severity Ubiquiti flaws exploited in attacks
items.description: The U.S. Cybersecurity and Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA) is warning of hackers actively exploiting flaws in Ubiquity UniFi OS and Lantronix serial-to-ethernet servers. [...]

items.title: Amadey, StealC malware operations disrupted in Operation Endgame action
items.description: Microsoft, Europol, and international partners have disrupted infrastructure used by the Amadey and StealC malware operations as part of Operation Endgame, which targets cybercriminal services and ransomware gangs. [...]

items.title: German rail services resume after wireless communications outage
items.description: Deutsche Bahn said a nationwide disruption of railway services was tied to a malfunction in its 2G-based GSM-R communications system.

items.title: Securing the service desk: Why social engineering attacks keep succeeding
items.description: Service desks have become a favored target for attackers seeking password resets, MFA changes, and access to corporate accounts. Specops Software breaks down how service desk social engineering attacks work and how organizations can defend against them. [...]

items.title: Indian auto giant Bajaj Auto hit by ransomware incident
items.description: The company said in a regulatory filing that it became aware of the incident on Tuesday morning and had taken precautionary measures to contain its impact.

items.title: Cordyceps CI/CD Flaws Expose 300+ GitHub Repositories to Supply-Chain Attacks
items.description: Cybersecurity researchers have flagged a new class of CI/CD workflow weakness that allows attackers to hijack workflows and compromise open-source supply chains. The "critical exploitable pattern" has been codenamed Cordyceps by Novee Security. The issue can allow full attacker control of repositories at dozens of the largest organizations worldwide, including Microsoft, Google, Apache, and

items.title: Apple's MacOS Gap Lets Users Disable Security Tools
items.description: Attackers can exploit the issue to disable security and integrated browser tools without needing administrator privileges or kernel exploits.

items.title: Dawn of the Apex Agentic Adversary
items.description: We are standing at the end of an era we never thought to mourn: the era of human-speed threats. For years, cybersecurity moved to a rhythm organizations could follow. A researcher found a bug, a CVE was cataloged, a vendor navigated a patch cycle, and weeks or even months later, a fix was deployed. In this era, dwell time was measured in days, sometimes weeks. We are now approaching an

items.title: Stealthy Mistic backdoor linked to ransomware access broker KongTuke
items.description: A new backdoor dubbed Mistic has been observed in financially motivated attacks targeting organizations in the insurance, education, IT, and professional services sectors. [...]

items.title: DoJ Seizes Huione Cloud Account Tied to Cyber Scam Money Laundering
items.description: The U.S. Department of Justice (DoJ) on Tuesday announced the seizure of a cloud computing account put to use by subsidiaries of Cambodia-based corporate conglomerate HuiOne Group, as the Treasury unveiled fresh sanctions against nine individuals and 26 entities linked to Prince Group. "These subsidiaries are alleged to have assisted individuals and organizations in transferring proceeds of

items.title: Linux Process Name Masquerading, (Wed, Jun 24th)
items.description: In a previous diary, I talked about stack strings&&#x23;x26;&#x23;x5b;1&&#x23;x26;&#x23;x5d; with a practical example of them. Since my SEC670 class, I&&#x23;x26;&#x23;xe2;&&#x23;x26;&#x23;x80;&&#x23;x26;&#x23;x99;m even more interested&&#x23;x26;&#x23;xc2;&&#x23;x26;&#x23;xa0;in malware obfuscation techniques. I had&&#x23;x26;&#x23;xc2;&&#x23;x26;&#x23;xa0;a look at process names. When you list running processes on a computer, can you trust what you see&&#x23;x26;&#x23;x3f; If you&&#x23;x26;&#x23;39;re facing a rootkit, malicious processes can be simply hidden (the API calls or commands to list processed have been tampered). But a malicious process&&#x23;x26;&#x23;xc2;&&#x23;x26;&#x23;xa0;can also mimic a non-suspicious name by

items.title: Cisco Unified CM flaw CVE-2026-20230 now exploited in attacks
items.description: A high-severity SSRF vulnerability, tracked as CVE-2026-20230, in Cisco Unified Communications Manager Server is now being exploited in attacks. [...]

items.title: Scope of Salesforce Attacks Expands as Icarus Leaks Data
items.description: More victims have emerged after attackers breached application vendor Klue and used its OAuth tokens to steal customers' Salesforce data.

items.title: 'Cordyceps': Mushrooming Malicious Pull Requests Threaten Developer Workflows
items.description: The CI/CD workflow weakness affects Microsoft's Azure Sentinel, Google's AI Agent Development Kit, Apache's Doris analytics database, Cloudflare's Workers SDK, and Python Software Foundation's Black.

items.title: Scattered Spider Hackers Plead Guilty on Day 1 of Trial
items.description: Two men pleaded guilty in the United Kingdom this week to criminal charges stemming from an August 2024 cyberattack that crippled Transport for London, the entity responsible for the public transport network in the Greater London area. The duo were key members of a prolific cybercrime group known as Scattered Spider, and their guilty pleas came on the first day of what was expected to be a six-week trial.

items.title: SocGholish Takedown Highlights Malicious TDS Threats
items.description: SocGholish uses traffic distribution systems (TDSs) to provide initial access into victims' networks for cybercrime groups such as the notorious Evil Corp.

items.title: FortiBleed Attackers Turn Firewalls Into Credential Stealers as Heists Persist
items.description: The threat actors engineered a Golang-based sniffer to target 430,000 FortiGate firewalls and identify 110 million credentials in the ongoing global campaign.

items.title: CVE-2024-40766: The Patch Fixed the Bug. Nobody Fixed the Configuration., (Tue, Jun 23rd)
items.description: The vulnerability

items.description: (c) SANS Internet Storm Center. https://isc.sans.edu Creative Commons Attribution-Noncommercial 3.0 United States License.

items.title: Webshells Remain Popular, (Mon, Jun 22nd)
items.description: Webshells have been popular for a long time. We already covered this topic across multiple diaries[1][2]. I spent some time to track them[3] and slighly paid less attention to them but today I found another one. It seems to be a new player (pushed on Github two months ago).

items.description: (c) SANS Internet Storm Center. https://isc.sans.edu Creative Commons Attribution-Noncommercial 3.0 United States License.

items.title: eBanking Phishing Delivered Through IPv4-Mapped IPv6 Address, (Fri, Jun 19th)
items.description: I detected an interesting phishing email this morning. It targets a major Belgian bank:

items.title: ‘Popa’ Botnet Linked to Publicly-Traded Israeli Firm
items.description: For the past four years, a sprawling Android-based botnet called Popa has forced millions of consumer TV boxes to relay Internet traffic linked to advertising fraud, account takeovers, and mass data-scraping efforts. This week, researchers from multiple security firms concluded that the Popa botnet is linked to NetNut, a "residential proxy" provider operated by the publicly-traded Israeli firm Alarum Technologies Ltd [NASDAQ: ALAR].

items.title: ISC Stormcast For Thursday, June 18th, 2026 https://isc.sans.edu/podcastdetail/9978, (Thu, Jun 18th)
items.description: (c) SANS Internet Storm Center. https://isc.sans.edu Creative Commons Attribution-Noncommercial 3.0 United States License.

items.title: The Behavior of Coordinated SSH Brute Force Attacks over the last three months [Guest Diary], (Wed, Jun 17th)
items.description: [This is a Guest Diary by Adam Nason, an ISC intern as part of the SANS.edu BACS program]

items.title: Who Runs the Ransomware Group ‘The Gentlemen?’
items.description: A cybercrime group known as The Gentlemen has emerged as the second most active ransomware gang by victim count, rapidly attracting a talented pool of hackers through an aggressive recruitment strategy that promises affiliates 90 percent of any ransom paid by victims. This post examines clues pointing to a real life identity for the administrator of The Gentlemen ransomware group.

items.title: A Record-Breaking Patch Tuesday for June 2026
items.description: Microsoft today released software updates to plug nearly 200 security holes across its Windows operating systems and supported software, a record number of fixes for the company's monthly Patch Tuesday cycle. Nearly three dozen of those bugs earned Microsoft's most dire "critical" rating, and exploit code for at least three of the weaknesses is now publicly available.

items.title: Hackers Used Meta’s AI Support Bot to Seize Instagram Accounts
items.description: The Instagram accounts for the Obama White House and the Chief Master Sergeant of the U.S. Space Force were briefly defaced with pro-Iranian images and messages over the weekend, after instructions began circulating on Telegram showing how to trick Meta's "AI support assistant" bot into resetting account passwords.

items.title: Netherlands Seizes 800 Servers, Arrests 2 for Aiding Cyberattacks
items.description: Authorities in the Netherlands have arrested the co-owners of two related Internet hosting companies for operating IT infrastructure used by Russia to carry out cyberattacks, influence operations and disinformation campaigns inside the European Union. The two men were the focus of a 2025 KrebsOnSecurity story about how their hosting companies had assumed control over the technical infrastructure of Stark Industries Solutions, an Internet service provider sanctioned last year by the EU as a frequent staging ground for cyber mischief from Russia's intelligence agencies.

items.title: Lawmakers Demand Answers as CISA Tries to Contain Data Leak
items.description: Lawmakers in both houses of Congress are demanding answers from the U.S. Cybersecurity & Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA) after KrebsOnSecurity reported this week that a CISA contractor intentionally published AWS GovCloud keys and a vast trove of other agency secrets on a public GitHub account. The inquiry comes as CISA is still struggling to contain the breach and invalidate the leaked credentials.

items.title: Alleged Kimwolf Botmaster ‘Dort’ Arrested, Charged in U.S. and Canada
items.description: Canadian authorities on Wednesday arrested a 23-year-old Ottawa man on suspicion of building and operating Kimwolf, a fast spreading Internet-of-Things botnet that enslaved millions of devices for use in a series of massive distributed denial-of-service (DDoS) attacks over the past six months. KrebsOnSecurity publicly named the suspect in February 2026 after the accused launched a volley of DDoS, doxing and swatting campaigns against this author and a security researcher. He now faces criminal hacking charges in both Canada and the United States.

items.title: CISA Admin Leaked AWS GovCloud Keys on Github
items.description: Until this past weekend, a contractor for the Cybersecurity & Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA) maintained a public GitHub repository that exposed credentials to several highly privileged AWS GovCloud accounts and a large number of internal CISA systems. Security experts said the public archive included files detailing how CISA builds, tests and deploys software internally, and that it represents one of the most egregious government data leaks in recent history.

items.title: Patch Tuesday, May 2026 Edition
items.description: Artificial intelligence platforms may be just as susceptible to social engineering as human beings, but they are proving remarkably good at finding security vulnerabilities in human-made computer code. That reality is on full display this month with some of the more widely-used software makers -- including Apple, Google, Microsoft, Mozilla and Oracle -- fixing near record volumes of security bugs, and/or quickening the tempo of their patch releases.

items.title: Student Loan Breach Exposes 2.5M Records
items.description: 2.5 million people were affected, in a breach that could spell more trouble down the line.

items.title: Watering Hole Attacks Push ScanBox Keylogger
items.description: Researchers uncover a watering hole attack likely carried out by APT TA423, which attempts to plant the ScanBox JavaScript-based reconnaissance tool.

items.title: Tentacles of ‘0ktapus’ Threat Group Victimize 130 Firms
items.description: Over 130 companies tangled in sprawling phishing campaign that spoofed a multi-factor authentication system.

items.title: Ransomware Attacks are on the Rise
items.description: Lockbit is by far this summer’s most prolific ransomware group, trailed by two offshoots of the Conti group.

items.title: Cybercriminals Are Selling Access to Chinese Surveillance Cameras
items.description: Tens of thousands of cameras have failed to patch a critical, 11-month-old CVE, leaving thousands of organizations exposed.

items.title: Twitter Whistleblower Complaint: The TL;DR Version
items.description: Twitter is blasted for security and privacy lapses by the company’s former head of security who alleges the social media giant’s actions amount to a national security risk.

items.title: Firewall Bug Under Active Attack Triggers CISA Warning
items.description: CISA is warning that Palo Alto Networks’ PAN-OS is under active attack and needs to be patched ASAP.

items.title: Fake Reservation Links Prey on Weary Travelers
items.description: Fake travel reservations are exacting more pain from the travel weary, already dealing with the misery of canceled flights and overbooked hotels.

items.title: iPhone Users Urged to Update to Patch 2 Zero-Days
items.description: Separate fixes to macOS and iOS patch respective flaws in the kernel and WebKit that can allow threat actors to take over devices and are under attack.

items.title: Google Patches Chrome’s Fifth Zero-Day of the Year
items.description: An insufficient validation input flaw, one of 11 patched in an update this week, could allow for arbitrary code execution and is under active attack.
